title: Calling Uniface Components from Java
text: Calling Uniface Components from Java You can use the Java Call-In API to call the operations of a Uniface component from your Java application and to configure the environment in which the components run. The easiest way to do this is to use the Configuration Manager JavaBean and the generated JavaBeans for Uniface components. It is also possible to use the wrapper classes directly, or even to implement your own call-in code using the UnifaceComponent base class and the methods it provides. Import the Java Call-in API and generated classes into your Java development tool. Import the ujci30.jar located in the Uniface /common/bin directory. This contains the classes for the Java Call-In API and the Configuration Manager. Import the .jar files containing the generated and compiled Java files for the Uniface components into your Java development tool. For more information, see Generating and Compiling Java Wrapper Files . In your Java code, import the package com.rocketsoftware.uniface.jci : import com.rocketsoftware.uniface.jci.*; Copy To use the JavaBeans, add them to your application in your preferred development environment. The ConfigManagerBean must be added to the application first, followed by the generated component beans. To create an instance of a Uniface component, use the Java new operator and the Java class representing the Uniface component. To a Java application, Uniface is a class factory, capable of creating instances for new components. Use the following constructor: ComponentClassName ( Properties instProp) ComponentClassName —name of the Java class representing the Uniface component. instProp —list of component instance properties. By default, the property component_id is provided by the generated Java file, and is used to compare the version of the generated Java file with the component signature in Uniface. Tip: During development, it can be useful to disable component ID checking, in which case you can specify an empty string as component ID. For example: //Construct a Properties object Properties instProps = new Properties(); Copy // Set the value of component_id to empty to // disable component ID checking for the component. instProps.put ("component_id", ""); Copy // Create a new instance of the Product component Product m = new Product (instProps); Copy This causes Uniface to execute the newinstance Proc Script command. To call operations on Uniface components, use the methods available in the UnifaceComponent base class. The generated Java wrapper classes for Uniface components are derived from the UnifaceComponent base class, so the following methods are available: The accept , exec , remove , and quit methods call predefined operations that execute Uniface triggers. Other methods implement the activate method to call other operations on the component. To get information about the available operations and their parameters, examine the generated .java source files. The activate method can also be used if you are writing your own code to activate Uniface component For example, the generated Product.java file defines the method getPrice which calls the get_price operation: /** Operation get_price. * * @param item_name string : in * @param item_price numeric : out * @param item_available boolean : out*/ public void getPrice (String itemName, IntHolder itemPrice, BooleanHolder itemAvailable) throws UnifaceException { Object params[] = new Object[4]; params[1] = itemName activate ("get_price", params); if (params[2] != null) itemPrice.value = ((Integer)params[2]).intValue() if (params[3] != null) itemAvailable.value = ((Boolean)params[3]).booleanValue(); } Copy To prepare and use operation parameters, you can use the UnifaceDateTime , UnifaceEntity , and UnifaceOccurrence classes. For more information, see Parameters and Data Type Mapping Between Uniface and Java Related Links Java Call-in API The Java Call-In API allows Java applications to call operations of Uniface components through the Uniface Universal Request Broker (URB), which maps Java method-calling conventions and data types to the calling standards and data types used by Uniface.
